1916 flood level. Pre Benalla Weir construction. The water flow rate was about 1/3 less than the 1993 flood.

October 1993 flood level and 1% flood level (height shown on map pg 2). There is a 1% chance of a flood this size occurring in any year. 1993 was the largest flood since records began. Most of Benalla, Baddaginnie and rural farm areas were significantly affected. Many people were evacuated from their homes. Many areas experienced floodwater travelling backwards through the stormwater drains, unexpectedly affecting many homes and properties, eg. in the Thomas St area. If a flood of this height happened today, over 870 homes and 210 businesses are likely to experience above floor flooding.
About 361 homes and 138 businesses are likely to be affected by above floor flooding.
June 1917 Flood level. Before Lake Benalla and Benalla Weir’s construction.

The levee has no freeboard remaining and is likely to be overtopped if water rises. Levees can fail before this level.
1% flood level. The flow path between the Castle and Sevens Creek via Brock and Anderson Street likely to experience significant flows.
Euroa Main Road is likely to have a depth of up to 150 millimetres along a stretch of around 300 metres of road.

Water is likely to get deeper upstream of Euroa Main Road and the railway embankment. Euroa Main Road is likely to have a depth of up to 100 millimetres on it. Castle and Seven Creeks are likely to begin interaction with a flow path forming via Brock Street and Euroa- Shepparton Road near Wood Road.
Water is likely to overtop Euroa Main Road and further inundate the floodplain.

October 2023 flood event

◼ Sydenham Inlet Road becomes unpassable. ◼ Properties or areas at the end of Sydenham Parade can become isolated or impacted by flood water. ◼ Floodwater breaches the banks to the East of the Bemm River onto the land between Sydenham Inlet Road and Sydenham Parade, including Bobs Bay and Cozy Nook Flats. During a flood that is larger than 3.2m; ◼ Sydenham Inlet Road becomes unpassable. ◼ Flooding extends from the Bemm River Bridge to the southern point of the flood plain and across to Bobs Bay. ◼ Riverside camp areas and some low lying caravan parks impacted. ◼ Relocation of stock required in some cases. Although no two floods are the same and the environment is constantly changing, the below table provides an idea of what to expect at different flood heights. ◼ Before a flood: Use the floodwater heights in the table to help you decide when you will need to act to protect yourself and your property in a flood event. ◼ During a flood: When a warning is issued by VicEmergency, use the predicted floodwater height and warning information to understand what the potential impacts may be for you and your community. *The height measurement is at the Bemm River upstream gauge. ^This table includes information from previous flood events and flood studies, which are scientific models used to predict impacts at different flood heights and flows. What roads are likely to flood? During a flood, the roads listed above may flood and could be closed. For current road closures, check VicTraffic. VicEmergency may include road closures in its warnings, see page 7 for more information. Stay safe by never driving through floodwater. It can take just 15cm of flowing water to float a car, that’s the height of an average pen.
◼ Floodwater on the Sydenham Inlet Road rises to a depth greater than 0.3 meters. ◼ The floodwater flow speed and depth increases and limits access of Sydenham Inlet Road.

◼ Floodwater covers large areas southeast of Pardew Track affecting agricultural properties and livestock. ◼ Areas of the Sydenham Inlet Road between Bemm River and Bemm River Bridge flood up to 0.3 meters.

Potential Impacts: ◼ Widespread flooding. ◼ Many houses and businesses are inundated above floor level. ◼ Properties and whole areas may be isolated. ◼ Major roads and rail routes closed. ◼ Many evacuations required. ◼ Impact to utility services (water, power, sewerage) Cann River Flood Class Levels and Potential Impacts The Minor, Moderate and Major flood class level categories are standards set across Australia by the Bureau of Meteorology to provide consistent understanding about the consequences and impacts of flooding in the table below. Monitor the water levels of these gauges at the Bureau of Meteorology website: Latest Rivers Heights at Cann River

Potential Impacts: ◼ Water spilling over riverbanks and covering large areas of land. ◼ Water above floor level in some houses and buildings. ◼ Evacuation may be required in some areas. ◼ Impact to roads and traffic routes. Major

1% AEP Height, see page 4. 1952 flood height. • Areas of the Princes Highway, Golf Links Road, Roadknight Street, Palmers Road and Bullock Island Road are affected by flood water. • Flooding in areas below expands further and increases in depth, affecting most of the Lakes Entrance Peninsula and Eastern Beach area.
• Most areas on the Lakes Entrance Peninsula and the Eastern Beach area are affected by flood water, affecting up to 400 homes and isolating 250 properties.

Major flood level 2007 and 1998 flood event level. • Flooding in the areas below expands further and increases in depth potentially isolating properties and affecting businesses and travel through Lakes Entrance. • Low lying areas of Carpenter Street, Short Street, Jetty Road, Middle Street, Long Street, Fish Street and Marrungbaur Street may be affected by flood.

• Several areas of The Esplanade and Marine Parade and most of Laura Street, Carstairs Street, Box Street, Gray Street, Rowe Street, Bulmer Street and Stock Street may be affected by flood water. • Marine Parade Reserve Playground, Lions Park at Eastern Beach Road, Lakes Entrance Skate Park and Woolworths Carpark may be affected by flood water.

Minor flood level • Marine Parade, The Esplanade, Laura Street, Carstairs Avenue, Gray Street, Mechanics Street, Staunton Street and Devitt Street may have localised areas of flooding at storm water drains.

• Low lying areas including boat ramps, jetties, carparks, walking tracks and parks near water can experience flooding. • Boat mooring lines may need to be extended. Major flood level The roads and areas impacted by flooding continue to experience rises in floodwater and affecting a larger area, with some areas having depths of 1.2m of dangerously fast flowing floodwater.
• Marlo-Cabbage Tree Road at Emu Creek is affected by flood water. • Old Station Road and Beach Road at Ewings Marsh are affected by floodwater.

Moderate flood level • Marlo Road between Snaggers Lane and Marlo township is affected by floodwater • Buchan-Orbost Road between Burn Street and Bete Belong Road • Forrest Road between Marlo Road and Macleod Road including Forest Park • The Orbost Snowy River Caravan Park at Nicholson Street is flooded.

• Low lying areas of Marlo Road are affected by flood water up to 0.8m deep. • Corringle Road south of Ewings Marsh Road is affected by flood water up to 0.8m deep restricting access to the campground and Marlo Coastal Reserve. • Tabbara Road south of Sandy Flat Road is affected by floodwater. • Old Marlo Road and Beach Road, Marlo are affected by floodwater and boat ramps are inaccessible.
The Snowy River continues to extend across the floodplains at Bete Belong, Jarrahmond and between Orbost and Marlo at depths up to 1.2 metres including;

Minor flood level The Snowy River begins to break out of its channel into low lying areas of the floodplains and drains at Jarrahmond and south of Orbost near the Brodribb River and Lake Curlip, with some areas having a 2 metre depth. • B Road (including Bouchers Lane) between Forrest Road and Nixons Road is closed due to flood water. • Buchan-Orbost Road between Bete Belong Road and Deaseys Cutting Road is affected by floodwater. • Jarramond Road between Guns and Huxters Road is affected by flood water. • Low lying areas on Lochend Road, Sandy Flat Road and Healeys are affected by floodwater. • Walking and cycling tracks are affected by floodwater.

The Tambo River and the Swifts Creek have overflowed into a wider area throughout the Tambo Valley and Ensay affecting many homes, properties and travel routes and potentially isolating Swifts Creek. Great Alpine Road and Swifts Creek East Road are closed at: • Simpson Creek (approximately 2km south of Swifts Creek) • Old Sheep Station Creek (1km north of Ensay-Doctors Flat Road intersection) • Bald Hills Creek Road • North of Angora Range Road The following roads are also impacted by flooding: • McMillan Street bridge, and • Hollonds Road and Obriens Road bridge are closed • McLarty Road at the southend • Sheltons Lane • Cassilus Road Bridge near Rileys Creek Road • Ensay-Doctors Flat Road at Great Alpine Road, Ensay. • Bindi Road near the Great Alpine Road intersection • Nunniong Road at Old Bindi Road intersection • Sandy Creek Road bridge

The Tambo River is starting to overflow into low lying areas. • The Hollonds Road and Obriens Road bridge over the Tambo River at Swifts Creek is starting to flood. • Nunniong Road bridge at the Tambo River in Bindi is blocked by water across the road. • Sandy Creek Road Bridge at the Tambo River in Ensay is closed. • Millers Access Road at Ensay has water over the road.

impacts.impact: 1% Annual Exceedance Possibility, see page 4. • Bairnsdale-Dargo Road is unpassable between Bon Accord Road and Cowells Lane. • Low lying areas of Marshalls Road, Woodglen Road and Perry Road have water across the road, potentially isolating businesses and properties. • The Mitchell River Flats are flooded with most areas having fast flowing water. *The height measurement is at the Mitchell River at Glenaladale gauge. ^This table includes information flood studies, which are scientific models used to predict impacts at different flood heights and flows.

• Bairnsdale-Dargo Road at Windmill Road is unpassable due to water across the road. • Fernbank-Glenaladale Road at the Iguana Creek has water across the road, potentially isolating the Glenaladale township. • The Mitchell River Flats are now completely flooded with some areas having fast flowing water.

• Wy Yung-Calulu Road at Boggy Creek Bridge is unpassable due to water over the road, and Lindenow-Glenaladale Road has more areas affected by flood water. • Windmill Road, Settlement Road, Thatchers Lane, Bon Accord Road, Lubys Lane, Alexanders Road and Baudinetts Road between Scotts Road and Hillside Road have water over the road, possibly isolating properties. • Water covers a greater extent of the Mitchell River Flats with some areas having flowing water.

• Lindenow-Glenaladale Road at Rodericks Road and the Calulu Bridge and Fernbank -Glenaladale Road at Moilun Creek are unpassable due to water across the road. • Low lying areas of Soldiers Road, Bulmers Road, Ross Street and Hansfords Lane have water over the road and flood water is affecting farmland in Hillside. • Water is breaking out of the river channels and expanding across the low lying areas of the Mitchell River Flats and wetlands.

January 2024 Flood Event

June, 2007 flood event. ◼ The largest flood on record for the Macalister Catchment was experienced after record rainfall fell throughout Gippsland with Murderers Hill recording 222mm of rain in a 24 hour period. ◼ Lake Glenmaggie rose from 50% storage to spilling over through the night.
◼ 1% AEP Height, which means there is a 1% chance of this size flood (or greater) happening in any year.

October, 2023 flood event. ◼ The catchment received more than 174mm of rain in a 24 hour period causing major flood warnings notifying the townships of Maffra, Riverslea and Bundalaguah to evacuate the area or move to higher ground.
June, 2012 flood event. ◼ In some areas of Gippsland more than 200mm of rain fell causing major flooding in most of the Gippsland catchments, impacting major roads and isolating towns.
Major flood level
August 2011 flood event.
Moderate flood level
October 1996 flood event.
Minor flood level

April 1990 flood event. ◼ Large amounts of rain fell in the upper Macalister catchment and resulted in Lake Glenmaggie storage levels increasing from 11% to 90% capacity by receiving 113,000 ML per day. ◼ Southern Rural Water commenced controlled releases as part of mitigating the flood down stream, which successfully reduced the flood effects for Maffra, Riverslea and Bundalaguah, and also down further at Sale.

June, 2007 flood event. ◼ The largest flood on record for the Macalister Catchment was experienced after record rainfall fell throughout Gippsland with Murderers Hill recording 222mm of rain in a 24 hour period. ◼ Lake Glenmaggie went from 50% storage to spilling over through the night. This event isolated townships including Newry and Tinamba, and 91 residents were winched and airlifted by helicopter, or rescued by boat from the area.

October, 2023 flood event. ◼ The catchment received more than 150mm of rain in a 24 hour period causing the Macalister River to rise quickly needing urgent major flood warnings notifying the townships of Newry and Tinamba to evacuate.
June, 2012 flood event. ◼ In some areas of Gippsland more than 200mm of rain fell causing major flooding in most of the Gippsland catchments, impacting major roads and isolating towns.

March 2012 and 1916 flood levels. In 2012 over 160 buildings were damaged above floor level. The Shepparton-Numurkah railway line was badly damaged. 130 local roads were closed including the Goulburn Valley Highway, Katamatite Road and Numurkah-Nathalia Road isolating many properties. Floodwater from the Muckatah Depression added to flooding in Numurkah, Yarrawonga, Burramine South, Katatmatite East and Naring.

May 1974 flood level. During this flood very heavy rain fell across local catchments of the Muckatah Depression, the Boosey, Broken, Nine Mile, Wild Dog and Box Creeks. The Broken, Nine Mile and Box Creeks all merged and flooding lasted nearly two months. Wild Dog and Box Creeks significantly added to flooding upstream of Numurkah. Commerical and residential areas flooded with 7 buildings damged by flood water. Major sandbagging efforts by locals saved the town from more damage.

October 1993 flood level. This flood was caused by heavy rainfall across the Broken River area near Benalla causing significant flooding and damage to rural properties. No major effects in the Numurkah township. Katamatite-Nathalia Road was closed.
Estimated 1939 flood Ievel

More than 5 buildings may be impacted above floor in Rupanyup. More than 1103 properties are impacted by flooding and may houses are isolated along Dunmunkle Creek.

During the January 2011 flood event Rupanyup was impacted by stormwater (localised) and riverine flooding from Dunmunkle Creek causing significant damage to buildings, roads and other infrastructure. Over 149mm fell over 3 days. More than 18 buildings were threatened by flooding. Due to extensive sandbagging only 7 buildings were flooded above floor. Most of the roads in Rupanyup were impacted by flooding. Flood mitigation works undertaken since this flood event has significantly reduced flood risk in Rupanyup (refer to the mitigation works summary on page 4).
Flooding may cut access to 17 buildings along Dunmunkle Creek, more than 937 properties may be impacted by flooding.

Deep flooding may cut access to the Wimmera Highway (north of Rupanyup) and Stawell-Warracknabeal Road (north of Rupanyup), Boyds Road and McIntyres Road. One building may be flooded above floor. Floodwater may break out from Dunmunkle Creek through the Rupanyup Golf Course, north of Rupanyup (0.5m depth).Flooding may cut access to 16 buildings along Dunmunkle Creek, more than 905 properties may be impacted by flooding.

Shallow flooding starts to overtop the Wimmera Highway (north of Rupanyup) and Stawell-Warracknabeal Road (north of Rupanyup). Deep flooding may cut access to Connolly Parade and Ron Lingham Drive. Flooding may cut access to 13 buildings along Dunmunkle Creek, more than 738 properties may be impacted by flooding.
Flooding may reach Rupanyup. Shallow flooding may begin impact roads along Dunmunkle Creek, these include Stawell-Warracknabeal Road, Horsham-Lubeck Road and C Readings Road.

Flooding along Dunmunkle Creek is driven by high flows in the Wimmera River. Floodwater breaks out into Dunmunkle Creek above the minor flood level. Along the Dunmunkle Creek several stock and domestic channels influence drainage and floodwater.
Minor flooding along low lying land and minor roads along the Wimmera River.

Moderate flood level Low lying agricultural farmland starts to flood. Water over road Docker-Carboor Road is closed between Wangaratta to Whitfield Road and the Oxley to Meadow Creek Road
Minor flood level No known impacts at this level.
Community infrastructure that may be impacted by floodwater: ◼ Warburton Christian School, Warburton Highway ◼ Warburton Sports Ground
Community infrastructure that may be impacted by floodwater: ◼ Yarra River walking trail at Warburton Christian School floods. Water over road: ◼ Kellys Road, Warburton
Community infrastructure that may be impacted by floodwater: ◼ Warburton Holiday Park starts to flood.

January, 2011 flood event. (Height shown on map page 4) ◼ During this flood a significant number of properties were affected by floodwater. ◼ At this height, many roads are flooded including the Borung Highway, Kelsall Street and Warracknabeal-Jeparit Road. The Henty Highway will be impacted.
◼ Apex Park and most of the Warracknabeal Caravan Park is inundated. ◼ Warracknabeal-Jeparit Road is closed by floodwater.

◼ The creek breaks its banks across Cemetery Road and Warunda Avenue ◼ Edges of the caravan park begin to flood. ◼ Craig Avenue and a number of properties in the area are flooded below floor height. ◼ The creek end of Asquith Avenue begins to flood.

◼ Water over Cemetery Road near the creek. ◼ Flood water is mostly contained within the creek’s banks. Although no two floods are the same and the environment is constantly changing, the below table provides an idea of what to expect at different flood heights. ◼ Before a flood: Use the floodwater heights in the table to help you decide when you will need to act to protect yourself and your property in a flood event. ◼ During a flood: When a warning is issued by VicEmergency, use the predicted floodwater height and warning information to understand what the potential impacts may be for you and your community. *The height measurement is at the Warracknabeal Weir Gauge. ^This table includes information from previous flood events and flood studies, which are scientific models used to predict impacts at different flood heights and flows.
January, 2011 flood level (flood extent shown on map page 2) 11 buildings were flooded above floor including the Wickliffe Hotel and Cookie’s Old Store.
6 buildings are flooded above floor in Walker Street. The Glenelg Highway bridge, south side of Wickliffe is close to being overtopped.
3 buildings are flooded above floor, including the CFA Station. Flooding impacts 9 properties.

Two properties in Walker Street start to be impacted by flooding. No buildings are impacted by flooding above floor. The Glenelg Highway and Walker Street is impassable to traffic to the north of Wickliffe, flood depth up to 0.5m.
Shallow flooding overtops the Glenelg Highway to a depth up to 0.15m.
October 1986 flood event.
August 1992 flood event.

impacts.impact: 10 June 2021 flood peak level Flood event: ◼ 31 residents from Doon Reserve Caravan Park evacuated. ◼ Corduroy Road, Doon Road, Settlement Road and Lowes Road, Yarra Junction, flooded. Roads were blocked with widespread flooding to paddocks and yards ◼ Warburton Highway at Little Yarra River flooded.
Area shown on map, see page 4. 1% (or greater) chance in any year ◼ 10 properties maybe impacted at this level
